topic: Embedded - no_std with the alloc Crate

turns.content: I'm getting a linker error when I try to compile my proof-of-conceit. The message says undefined reference to 'core::panicking::panic_impl' and it seems like I forgot something about the global allocator in no_std environments.

That looks like you're missing a basic requirement for #![no_std] builds. In Rust, when you disable the standard library, the panic implementation isn't automatically available unless you explicitly provide one. You need to add extern crate alloc; to your crate root so that the core::panicking module gets linked properly without relying on std's runtime hooks.

I added extern crate alloc but it still fails. I thought maybe I needed to manually implement panic, or perhaps I misunderstood how the global allocator connects to Vec and String operations in this context?

You're right to suspect the connection between allocation and panics. In no_std land with a custom heap allocator like embedded-alloc, every data structure that owns memory—Vec, String, HashMap—must use your allocator's implementation of Drop and Panicking traits. The extern crate alloc; line is mandatory because it exports the core module which contains panic_impl. Without it, the compiler cannot find symbols for things like Vec::new or String::from_utf8, even if you've defined a heap_allocator.

Okay so I added the extern crate and now I can compile basic types but still have runtime issues when my program tries to allocate large buffers. It seems like embedded-alloc isn't being picked up correctly in the build script?

The issue is likely that you're not explicitly telling Cargo which allocator to use during compilation. Even with extern crate alloc;, if your Cargo.toml doesn't specify [build-dependencies] or uses cfg-if checks, the fallback might still be using std's allocator. You should declare embedded-alloc as a build-dependency in your Cargo.toml and ensure your main.rs explicitly imports it so that the heap_allocator trait is resolved before any Vec or String operations occur.

I updated Cargo.toml with [build-dependencies] but now I get a different error. It says 'no rule matches' for some function call. Is this because my custom allocator isn't implementing the required traits correctly?

Yes, that's exactly it. Your heap_allocator implementation must fully implement Drop and Panicking traits to replace std's behavior. If you're missing a method or using an incorrect trait name from embedded-alloc, the compiler won't know how to handle memory cleanup during drop. For example, if your allocator doesn't correctly implement core::panicking::Panic, then any panic attempt will fail at compile time because there's no target for the signal.

I checked my code and it looks correct. But I still can't get Vec to work with embedded-alloc without errors. Is there something specific about how the alloc crate interacts with the standard library types that I'm missing?

The key is understanding that extern crate alloc; only brings in core, not std's internal structs directly. When you use Vec or String, they rely on the allocator trait implemented by your heap_allocator. If your implementation doesn't correctly implement Drop and Panicking for the specific version of embedded-alloc you're using, those types won't compile. You might also need to ensure your build script generates the correct cfg flags so that no_std paths are taken instead of std ones.

Ah, I think I see it now. But wait, earlier you said extern crate alloc; is enough for panic_impl. Why do I still need this extra setup? Isn't the global allocator supposed to be automatic once I declare it?

You're catching a subtle distinction here. The extern crate alloc; line provides access to core::panicking but doesn't automatically configure your heap_allocator's interaction with Vec and String. Those types still need the custom Drop and Panicking traits wired up correctly in your allocator implementation. It's not just about linking the symbols; it's about ensuring every allocation path through your code respects the embedded-alloc contract.

topic: Embedded - no_std with the alloc Crate

turns.content: I've been away from Rust for five years and I'm trying to get back into embedded development. The book says I should use #![no_std] with extern crate alloc; but isn't this still running code on the heap? So when I try to use Vec or String in a no_std context, doesn that mean there's still an implicit allocator somewhere?

You've hit exactly the right spot. When you write #![no_std], you're telling the compiler not to link against the standard library, which means no global heap is automatically available for things like Vec or String. The alloc crate provides a minimal allocator that gives you access to these types without requiring a full OS environment, but it's still an external dependency you must explicitly include. So yes, using Vec or String with extern crate alloc; does mean there's a heap allocator running in your binary, just one provided by the crate itself rather than the system.

Okay so I understand that extern crate alloc gives me access to Vec and String, but what happens if my microcontroller doesn't have enough RAM for a full allocator? Can I just drop down to bare metal without any heap at all?

That's the next level of embedded Rust. If your target has very limited resources, you can indeed move beyond alloc and use crates like embedded-alloc which provide a heap allocator specifically designed for constrained environments with minimal overhead. These crates implement their own allocation logic that works even when you have no standard library features available. However, if you truly need zero heap usage, you'd be writing everything as static arrays or using panic! to signal errors instead of allocating dynamically.
So I'm confused about the distinction between just having no_std versus actually needing a custom allocator. Can you explain why someone would choose embedded-alloc over the standard alloc crate?

The main difference is efficiency and memory footprint. The standard alloc crate provides a basic heap allocator that works fine for many projects, but it might include unnecessary features or dependencies if you're working on very small embedded systems. Crates like embedded-alloc are built specifically to minimize the code size and RAM usage by providing lightweight allocation strategies tailored for constrained hardware. This is important because every byte counts when you're writing firmware for a microcontroller with limited flash and SRAM.
Got it, but what about safety? If I'm using Vec or String with an external allocator, how do I prevent buffer overflows since there's no compiler-enforced bounds checking like in std?

You're right to be concerned. When you use Vec or String without the standard library, you lose some of its automatic safety guarantees. The allocator itself doesn't enforce bounds checking, so it's up to your code to manage memory carefully. You can still get some safety by using traits like SafeDrop for cleanup and careful error handling with panic! when things go wrong. But for critical systems where security matters, you might prefer avoiding dynamic allocation altogether in favor of fixed-size buffers or manual memory management.
That makes sense. So if I'm working on a project that needs to store large amounts of data dynamically, should I stick with the standard alloc crate or look into more advanced heap allocators?

If your application requires dynamic storage of substantial data, starting with the standard alloc crate is usually a good choice because it's well-tested and widely supported. However, if you later discover performance or memory constraints become an issue, you can swap in more sophisticated heap allocators like embedded-alloc or others that offer better fragmentation management or smaller code footprints. The key is to profile your system early to know whether the basic allocator will suffice or if a custom solution is needed.
One more thing, I noticed some tutorials mention using Box instead of Vec for certain cases in no_std environments. What's the advantage there?

Box is useful when you need heap-allocated data but want to own it directly rather than manage a container like Vec. It allows you to store pointers to dynamically allocated resources, which can be handy for creating small pools of objects or managing ownership chains in complex systems. However, Box still relies on an allocator behind the scenes, so if your goal is truly zero heap usage, you'd need to avoid Box entirely and use static allocations instead.
I think I'm getting clearer on when to use which approach. But what about error handling? If my allocator runs out of space in no_std, how do I handle that gracefully without panicking?

In a no_std context without the standard library's panic! mechanism, you need to implement custom error handling. You can create your own Result-like types and use traits like std::error::Error or similar interfaces that don't rely on panics. Many embedded projects define custom enums for allocation failures and return them through function signatures so callers can check conditions explicitly instead of relying on unwinding the stack.
Thanks, this helps a lot with understanding how to balance flexibility and constraints in my next embedded project. I'll start by trying extern crate alloc; and see if I need to switch later.

That's exactly the right approach. Start simple with the standard allocator, profile your system for memory usage and performance, then consider swapping in a more specialized heap allocator like embedded-alloc only when you identify specific bottlenecks. Good luck with your return to Rust development!
